name: Curse Modifier
description: A harmful or complicating run modifier that changes rules, rewards, enemies, resources, or player options until removed or the run ends.
design_notes: Curses are most useful when they create new decisions instead of only reducing numbers. A curse should name its trigger, stack rule, removal condition, UI explanation, and interactions with relics, rooms, shops, and death policy. Designers should be able to audit which curses can combine before shipping a pool.
design_purpose: Add risk to powerful rewards, shortcuts, shrines, events, or difficulty escalation.
Create temporary or persistent pressure that changes route and build choices.
Give advanced players optional adversity with higher reward potential.
player_fantasy: The player feels tempted by power while carrying a dangerous consequence that reshapes the run.

parameters.name: curse_modifier_id
parameters.description: Stable curse identifier used by rules, UI, and save data.

parameters.name: modifier_stack_rule
parameters.description: Rule for applying a curse when a matching modifier already exists.

parameters.name: removal_condition_id
parameters.description: Condition that clears or transforms the active curse.

parameters.name: tension_budget_delta
parameters.description: Pacing adjustment used by directors after the curse is active.

parameters.name: reward_multiplier_profile_id
parameters.description: Optional reward profile granted for accepting the curse.

parameters.name: curse_visibility_policy_id
parameters.description: How much curse behavior is disclosed before acceptance.

edge_cases: A curse removes the only action, resource, or item needed to complete the current floor.
Two curses modify the same reward_pool_id and produce impossible or empty reward choices.
A curse is accepted in a shop or event and then the run is saved before UI state closes.
A removal condition fires during the same step that the curse applies its penalty.
A content update renames curse_modifier_id while old saves still contain the previous id.
common_bugs: Stacking curses multiply each other because modifiers are applied repeatedly during stat recompute.
Hidden curses trigger without any readable cause and feel like a random bug to the player.
Removal clears the visual icon but leaves the gameplay modifier in the run_state_snapshot.
Reward boosts remain active after the curse is removed or after the run ends.
Curse descriptions are generated from stale parameters and misstate the active penalty.
balancing_notes: A curse paired with a reward should change play style, not simply tax the player after the reward is already taken.
Stacking rules need caps or compatibility tags so curses do not combine into unwinnable states.
Hidden curses are more tense when they are foreshadowed by event text or environment cues.
accessibility_notes: Show curse effects in plain language with persistent access from the run HUD or pause screen.
Use icon, text, and audio cues for curse activation rather than color alone.
Offer reduced randomness or preview modes if hidden curses make learning inaccessible.

implementation_notes.unity: Represent curses as data assets with curse_modifier_id, stack rule, stat hooks, and removal conditions. Apply them through a deterministic modifier pipeline that records active modifiers in run_state_snapshot and exposes debug output for final stat values.
implementation_notes.godot: Store active curses in a run-state Resource keyed by curse_modifier_id. Emit modifier_added, modifier_changed, and modifier_removed signals so UI, encounter generation, and reward generation update from the same committed state.
implementation_notes.unreal: Gameplay Effects or Gameplay Tags can model curse modifiers, but the run subsystem should own stack validation, removal_condition_id checks, and replicated active curse state. Keep designer-facing debug traces for why each modifier applied.
implementation_notes.web: Use versioned JSON definitions and a pure reducer for active curse lists. Persist curse ids, stack counts, and removal progress in the run save so refreshes do not reapply rewards or lose penalties.

example_games.title: Slay the Spire
example_games.note: Uses curses as deck penalties tied to events, rewards, and risk choices.

example_games.title: Darkest Dungeon
example_games.note: Uses negative traits and afflictions that shape party decisions.
